claims.normalized_claim: In Mahanoy Area School District v. B.L. (2021) the Supreme Court held that a public school violated a student's First Amendment free-speech rights by punishing her for off-campus speech, and that a school's special authority to regulate student speech is diminished when the speech happens off campus.
evidence_locations: Opinion of the Court (Breyer, J.), holding
allowed_wording: The Court ruled the school went too far in punishing an off-campus post.
prohibited_wording: Schools can never punish student speech.
Students have no free speech at school.

claims.normalized_claim: Mahanoy v. B.L. was decided on June 23, 2021 by an 8-1 vote; the majority opinion was by Justice Stephen Breyer, Justice Alito concurred (joined by Gorsuch), and Justice Thomas was the lone dissenter.
allowed_wording: In an 8-1 decision in 2021, Justice Breyer wrote for the Court; Justice Thomas dissented alone.

claims.normalized_claim: The Court did not hold that schools can never regulate off-campus speech; it expressly left open categories such as serious bullying or harassment, threats, and cheating, and declined to announce a broad rule.
allowed_wording: The Court did not give students total immunity off campus; it left room for things like bullying, threats, and cheating.
prohibited_wording: Schools have no power over off-campus speech at all.

claims.normalized_claim: The decision built on Tinker v. Des Moines (1969) — that students do not 'shed their constitutional rights to freedom of speech or expression at the schoolhouse gate' — and Tinker's 'substantial disruption' standard, which the Court found was not met here.
allowed_wording: It built on the 1969 Tinker case: students keep free-speech rights at school, but schools can act against speech that substantially disrupts.

claims.normalized_claim: The student, Brandi Levy (anonymized in the case as 'B.L.'), was a 14-year-old high-school student and junior-varsity cheerleader at Mahanoy Area High School in Mahanoy City, Pennsylvania.
allowed_wording: The student, Brandi Levy, was a 14-year-old JV cheerleader in Mahanoy, Pennsylvania.
claims.notes: Use '14-year-old high-school student' (avoid pinning a grade label). She went public with the ACLU — safe to name.

claims.normalized_claim: On a weekend, off campus at a local convenience store, after she failed to make the varsity cheer team, she posted a Snapchat image of herself and a friend raising their middle fingers, with a profane caption venting frustration at school, softball, cheer, and 'everything.'
allowed_wording: Off campus on a weekend, she posted a Snapchat with raised middle fingers and a profane caption after being put on JV instead of varsity.
prohibited_wording: [Do NOT quote the explicit profanity verbatim in narration; describe it.]
claims.notes: ADVERTISER SAFETY: describe the profanity, never read it; censor on screen.

claims.normalized_claim: The post was sent to about 250 Snapchat friends; although Snaps disappear, it was screenshotted and reached the cheerleading coaches, and the school suspended her from the junior-varsity cheerleading team for a year.
allowed_wording: It went to about 250 friends, was screenshotted, reached the coaches, and the school suspended her from JV cheer for a year.

claims.normalized_claim: She and her parents, represented by the ACLU, sued the school district, and the courts — and ultimately the Supreme Court — ruled in her favor.
allowed_wording: She sued with the ACLU, and won — at every level, up to the Supreme Court.

claims.normalized_claim: The Court noted the actual disruption at school was minimal — a brief classroom discussion of a few minutes — far short of the 'substantial disruption' Tinker requires before a school may punish student speech.
allowed_wording: The only fallout at school was a few minutes of classroom chatter — not a substantial disruption.
claims.notes: Why the school lost: no substantial disruption.

findings: Durable hashes 2/3 (SRC-0002 blocked). Advertiser-safe: never read profanity (CLM-0006). Neutral school-vs-student.
